coredata.dc:title: Rare earth-modified yellow carbon dots for long-term imaging in cells and zebrafish
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Kou, Xinyue
Cong, Yingge
Dong, Wen-Fei
Li, Li
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dot (CDs) materials are typically hindered by aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect. However, there are limitations in terms of their development and application. The introduction of aggregation-induced emission (AIE) characteristics into CDs has emerged as a viable solution to this challenge. In this work, we synthesized a novel yellow fluorescence (FL) rare earth-doped AIE CDs (named Y-CDs), which integrate the benefits of AIE materials, rare earth materials, and CDs. Y-CDs exhibit bright yellow FL, good temperature sensitivity and pH response properties. In cell imaging, Y-CDs showed excellent targeting capability towards the nuclear in HeLa cells. Y-CDs also showed excellent biocompatibility and safety in imaging of zebrafish embryos and zebrafish. Even on the 10th day following culture of zebrafish embryos, the digestive system and lens of zebrafish can be accurately located and imaged. The work presented here suggests the great potential of replacing commercial dyes with Y-CDs for highly specific nuclear labeling and bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
AIE
Temperature sensitivity
Nuclear targeting
Fluorescent imaging of zebrafish

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Steps to study the mechanism of endocytosis
Impact of doping of rare earth elements on Y-CDs FL
Temperature sensitivity and pH-responsive FL characteristics of Y-CDs
Cytotoxicity and intracellular imaging of Y-CDs
Application of Y-CDs in zebrafish imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Y-CDs. (a) TEM image of the Y-CDs in acetic acid. Scale bar is 20 nm. (b) TEM image of the Y-CDs in DMSO. Scale bar is 20 nm. (c) HR-TEM image of the Y-CDs in acetic acid. (inset: fast Fourier transform (FFT) diffraction). (d) HR-TEM image of the Y-CDs in DMSO. (inset: FFT diffraction). (e) XRD pattern of Y-CDs. (f) Raman spectra of Y-CDs. (g) FT-IR spectra of Y-CDs.
XPS analysis of the Y-CDs. (a) Full spectra of XPS analysis of the Y-CDs. (b) XPS spectrum high-resolution C 1s, (c) O 1s, (d) N 1s, (e) Yb 4d, (f) Er 4d spectra of Y-CDs.
(a) FL spectra of Y-CDs at 20-100 °C. (b) FL of Y-CDs at 525 nm with time. (c) FL spectra of Y-CDs at pH 1–9. (d) FL of Y-CDs as a function of pH at 525 nm.

(a) Confocal microscope FL image of Hela cells stained with Y-CDs. (b) DAPI and (c) Lyso-Tracker Green. (d) Merged confocal microscope FL images. Scale bar is 25 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Confocal microscope FL images (1,4), bright field images (2, 5) and merged images (3, 6) of zebrafish embryos and juvenile zebrafish incubated at 10 μg/mL Y-CDs for 30 min. (b) Confocal microscope FL images, bright field images and merged images of zebrafish embryos incubated with 10 μg/mL Y-CDs, 12 h (1, 7, 13), 1 day (2, 8, 14), 3 days (3, 9, 15), 5 days (4, 10, 16), 7 days (5, 11, 17), 10 days (6, 12, 18). YS: yolk sac; IN, intestine; IB, intestinal bulb; VE: vessel; EY: eye; LN: lens. (c) Confocal microscope FL images, bright field and merged images of zebrafish embryos incubated with Y-CDs at different concentrations for 14 days. (1–3) 10 μg/mL. OC: otic capsule (4–6) 100 μg/mL. Scale bar is 250 μm.

head.ce:title: Rare earth-modified yellow carbon dots for long-term imaging in cells and zebrafish

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of Life Sciences and Medicine
University of Science and Technology of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dot (CDs) materials are typically hindered by aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect. However, there are limitations in terms of their development and application. The introduction of aggregation-induced emission (AIE) characteristics into CDs has emerged as a viable solution to this challenge. In this work, we synthesized a novel yellow fluorescence (FL) rare earth-doped AIE CDs (named Y-CDs), which integrate the benefits of AIE materials, rare earth materials, and CDs. Y-CDs exhibit bright yellow FL, good temperature sensitivity and pH response properties. In cell imaging, Y-CDs showed excellent targeting capability towards the nuclear in HeLa cells. Y-CDs also showed excellent biocompatibility and safety in imaging of zebrafish embryos and zebrafish. Even on the 10th day following culture of zebrafish embryos, the digestive system and lens of zebrafish can be accurately located and imaged. The work presented here suggests the great potential of replacing commercial dyes with Y-CDs for highly specific nuclear labeling and bioimaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are an intriguing class of nanomaterials which have garnered a great deal of attention in recent years [1–4]. These carbon-based materials possess excellent fluorescence (FL) properties, which renders them highly appealing in a wide range of applications [5–8]. The exceptional FL properties of CDs can be attributed to factors such including quantum confinement, surface passivation, and the presence of functional groups on their nanoparticle surfaces [9]. Due to this versatility, CDs are promising candidates for bioimaging [10], drug delivery [11], sensing [12], and optoelectronics application [13]. In addition, CDs exhibit excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and notable photostability [14,15].

However, despite the fact that they possess excellent characteristics, CDs often encounter limitation of aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) effects [16]. In solid or aggregated state, the FL of CDs significantly decrease or even disappear entirely, this has limited the development and application of CDs.

In 2001, Tang and coworkers reported an intriguing phenomenon named aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effects [17]. This remarkable discovery served to demonstrate that some organic dyes exhibit vibrant fluorescence (FL) when they are in aggregated state, which was contrary to conventional understanding. The field has been revolutionized the field by the discovery of the AIE effect, which has opened up new possibilities for the design and development of luminous materials with enhanced performance. As a consequence, exploring the AIE effect in the context of CDs has emerged as an important research direction in the quest for high-performance light-emitting materials [18,19].

It is widely known that rare earth complexes have emerged as highly efficient luminescent materials recently and that they demonstrate, similarities to AIE agents in both solid and aggregated states [20,21]. However, one key distinction lies in the sensitized emission process that rare earth ions exhibit, which involvs the transfer of energy from the excited state of the ligand to the excited state of the rare earth ion (T1-Re*) [22], thereby exciting the rare earth ion. These ions possess exceptional luminescent properties, which include intense emission signals, prolonged FL lifetimes, and high quantum efficiency [23,24]. Despite these advantages, limited exploration of doping rare earth ions in AIE CDs. Due to the potential advantages of integrating rare earth components with AIE CDs, further investigation in this area has treat potential for the advancement of biological imaging applications.

This study synthesized a novel AIE CDs by introducing rare earth elements (Yb and Er), called Y-CDs. The maximum emission wavelength of Y-CDs was located at 525 nm with a maximum excitation wavelength of 440 nm. In addition, Y-CDs exhibited excellent optical properties, which included excitation-independent emission, pH-responsive and temperature-sensitive properties. Y-CDs were found to have low requirement in terms of incubation time, could achieve good imaging effect when incubation concentration was low, and demonstrated excellent targeting capability toward the nucleus in HeLa cells. Furthermore, in zebrafish and zebrafish embryos, the long-term imaging experiments in this study confirmed the biocompatibility and safety of Y-CDs while also selectively visualizing the crystalline and digestive systems of the zebrafish. This will serve to open a new window for building novel imaging labels.

The morphologies of the Y-CDs were analyzed using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM; JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan). X-ray diffractometry (XRD) patterns were obtained using a Bruker (Germany) diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained using a Kratos Axis Ultra system with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using a Fourier FT-IR spectrometer (PerkinElmer Inc., Waltham, MA, USA). Photoluminescence spectra were measured using a F-4600 spectrophotometer (Hitachi Japan). Cell and zebra fish imaging were performed using a Nikon A1+ Confocal laser FL microscope.

Tetraphenylethene (TPE), Yb(NO3)3, Er(NO3)3, acetic acid, and ethanol were purchased from Adamas Ltd., DMSO and DMF were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. DAPI dihydrochloride, WST-1, and 4 % paraformaldehyde, were obtained from the Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology. All other chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. Double-distilled water was used throughout all experiments.

To prepare the Y-CDs, TPE (332 mg), Yb(NO3)3 (129.2 mg), and Er(NO3)3 (21.3 mg) were dissolved in 40 mL acetic acid and subjected to sonication for 30 min. The resulting mixture was then transferred to an 80 mL Teflon-lined autoclave for solvothermal treatment at 180 °C for 16 h. Once the Y-CDs solution had cooled down to room temperature, deionized water was added in a 1:1 ratio, causing the immediate precipitation of a powder. The obtained troubled liquor was sonicated for 5 min and then centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. This centrifugation process was repeated three times to remove any residual solvent and starting materials. Finally, the solid powder was dried at 60 °C using a vacuum drying oven.
1 mL Y-CDs added deionized water in 1:1 ratio, water bath kettle used to adjust temperature (from 20 to 100 °C). The FL intensity of the samples at 525 nm with the excitation wavelength of 365 nm

1 mL Y-CDs added deionized water in 1:1 ratio, finely tuned to varied pH (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8 or 9) using phosphate buffer (10 mM) for each sample. The FL intensity of the samples at 525 nm with the excitation wavelength of 365 nm.

The cytotoxicity of Y-CDs was evaluated using the standard WST-1 method with HeLa cells, 4 T1 cells, and HEK293 cells. The cells were seeded in a 96-well plate and cultured for 24 h. After removing the original medium, 100 μL of fresh medium containing various concentrations of Y-CDs (ranging from 0 to 500 μg/mL) was added. The cells were then incubated for an additional 24 h. Following that, 20 μL of WST-1 reagent was added, and the cells were incubated for another 40 min. Cell viability was measured using a microplate reader at a wavelength of 450 nm. For the specific culture conditions, HeLa cells were cultured in a humidified incubator at 37 °C with 5 % CO2, with the medium supplemented with 10 % heat-inactivated FBS.

For cell imaging, HeLa cells were treated with various concentrations of the compound (ranging from 1 to 50 μg/mL) for different durations (ranging from 0.5 to 60 min). Subsequently, the cells were washed three times with PBS. After incubating for an additional hour, a DAPI paraformaldehyde solution was used for counterstaining, which lasted for 15 min. Finally, the cells were observed using confocal microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steps to study the mechanism of endocytosis
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the endocytosis mechanism of Y-CDs, the HeLa cells were separately pre-treated at low temperature (4 °C), 10 μg/mL of CPZ, 100 μg/mL of genistein, 10 μg/mL of MβCD, or 20 μg/mL of amiloride for 4 h. Next, the cells were incubated with 10 μg/mL of Y-CDs for 30 min and washed with PBS thrice. Finally, cells were observed by confocal microscopy.

The synthesis and characterization of Y-CDs were conducted using TPE, Yb(NO3)3, and Er(NO3)3 as the starting materials. The solvent thermal method in acetic acid, followed by a purification process were involved in the synthesis process. The schematic representation of the synthesis procedure for Y-CDs can be seen in Fig. 1 a.

Acetic acid played a crucial role during synthesis. When water was added to the Y-CDs solution, a phase transition occurred, which transformed the initially clear solution into a turbid liquid. This transition was accompanied by a significant shift from negligible FL to intense yellow FL, as shown in Fig. 1b. Further purification and drying processes resulted in solid material formation. The resulting yellow powder exhibited bright yellow FL under 365 nm ultraviolet (UV).

However, the yellow powder demonstrated poor dispersibility in water, which posed challenges for experimental procedures. To overcome this issue, DMSO solvent was used to dissolve the powder, which yielded a concentration of 1 mg/ml. Surprisingly, the addition of water in a 1:1 ratio result in an instant change from yellow solution to a yellow turbid liquid, fluoresced bright yellow under 365 UV was obtained.

TEM analysis was used to examine the morphology of Y-CDs. In Fig. 2 a and b, the TEM image shows a uniform distribution of Y-CDs with an approximate diameter of 5 nm when acetic acid was used as the solvent. Similarly, in the HR-TEM images (Fig. 2c and d), both acetic acid and DMSO solvents resulted in fine Y-CDs with interplanar spacing of 0.208 nm. These findings suggest that the as part of the optimization method following powder dissolution and purification in DMSO, the subsequent addition of water does not significantly affect the size or morphology of Y-CDs.

XRD analysis of Y-CDs revealed a distinct peak around 20°, which indicates the presence of a graphene structure, as can be seen in Fig. 2e. This peak is consistent with the findings og previous studies [25]. Raman spectroscopy, which is shown in Fig. 2f, exhibited two peaks at 1340 and 1590 cm−1, which corresponded to the D and G peak. The D peak is associated with lattice defects in carbon atoms, and the G peak represents the in-plane stretching vibration of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms [26,27]. The calculated ID/IG ratio of 0.821 suggests there was a higher proportion of sp2 hybridized graphite carbon atoms than disordered sp3 carbon atoms in Y-CDs [28].

FT-IR analysis (Fig. 2f) was performed to further characterize the functional groups on the surface of Y-CDs. The results show the presence of various functional groups or chemical bonds, including –OH (3440 cm−1), –NH (3100 cm−1), amide carbonyl (1682 cm−1), as well as CN (1589 cm−1) and CC (1462 cm−1), which provides evidence for the presence of nitrogen-containing groups in the synthesized Y-CDs [29].

XPS was used for investigating the chemical structure of Y-CDs in more detail. The XPS full spectrum analysis of Y-CDs (Fig. 3 a) revealed peaks at 533.08 eV, 284.8 eV, 406.11 eV, 200.1 eV, and 164.35 eV, which represented the proportions of C, O, N, Yb, and Er elements in Y-CDs, which are approximately 95.65 %, 3.82 %, 0.39 %, 0.1 %, and 0.04 %, respectively. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) that was obtained from high-resolution XPS analysis confirmed the presence of CO (284.8 eV) and CC (286.43 eV) bonds [30]. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) displayed three distinct peaks at 530.88 eV, 532.07 eV, and 534.18 eV, which correspond to lattice oxygen (OL), absorbed oxygen (OH), and oxygen vacancies (OV) [31]. In the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3d), peaks were observed at 399.19 eV, 400.65 eV, and 406.11 eV which represent CN, CN, and nitrate. This provides evidence for the successful incorporation of nitrogen dopants [32]. The Yb 4d region high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 3e) showed two peaks at 186.1 eV and 192.1 eV, which indicate the presence of Yb3+ species. Similarly, the Er 4d region high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 3f) showed a single peak at 168.35 eV, which confirms the presence of Er3+ ions [33]. These XPS findings serve to demonstrate the successful rare earth elements integration into Y-CDs.

UV–Vis absorption, FL excitation, and emission spectroscopy were used to characterize the optical properties of Y-CDs. The absorption spectrum revealed a prominent absorption band near 360 nm in the Y-CDs solution and the powder sample showed a broader absorption band in the 250 to 500 nm range. FL measurements showed a wide emission peak at around 525 nm for the Y-CDs solution, but there was relatively weak FL intensity. Similarly, the powder sample exhibited emission at 525 nm. It is notable that, the dispersed Y-CDs in solution showed no detectable FL, but a yellow FL emerged when aggregates were formed in a powder state (Fig. 4 a, b).

When the FL spectra of Y-CDs were examined under different excitation wavelengths, the FL signal was observed to progressively increased as the excitation wavelength ranged from 300 to 440 nm. The Y-CDs in an aggregated state exhibited optimal imaging results with emission at 525 nm at an excitation wavelength of 440 nm. However, increasing the excitation wavelength beyond 440 nm caused a significant decline in FL intensity (Fig. 4c). The above results demonstrate that Y-CDs possess excitation-independent properties.

The UV − Vis absorption, FL excitation, and emission of the as-prepared Y-CDs (a) the Y-CDs powder acetic acid solution. (b) Y-CDs powder. (c) FL spectra of Y-CDs aggregated states at different excitation wavelengths. (d) FL emission spectra of Y-CDs in different polar solvents. Inset shows the photographs of Y-CDs dissolved in different solvents under 365 nm UV(from left to right is acetic acid, ethanol, DMF, DMSO, and water). (e) The FL emission spectrum of Y-CDs solution varies with water content. The attached figure shows the photos of Y-CDs solution with water volume ratio from 0 to 90 % under daylight. (f) Trend of FL intensity of Y-CDs solution at 525 nm.

Y-CDs were dissolved in various solvents (ethyl, ethanol, DMF, DMSO, and water) at a concentration of 1 mg/ml to investigate the effects of different solvents on the FL of Y-CDs. The resulting fluorescent colors and emission spectra were recorded (Fig. 4d). Under 365 nm UV excitation, Y-CDs dispersed in water (ε = 10.2) exhibited vibrant yellow FL with an emission peak at 525 nm. When Y-CDs were dissolved in DMSO (ε = 7.2), they exhibited orange-red FL, although FL intensity was not particularly strong. The emission spectrum exhibited a peak at 470 nm and a lower shoulder peak at 620 nm. However, in the remaining solvents inculding DMF (ε = 6.4), acetic acid (ε = 6.2), and ethanol (ε = 4.3), the fluorescent signals were barely detectable. Changes in the FL spectrum of the Y-CDs solution (in DMSO) with different water content are shown in Fig. 4e. The illustration is the photograph of Y-CDs solution under 365 nm ultraviolet radiation, where the volume ratio of water varied between 10 % and 90 %. As water content increased from 10 % to 60 %, the FL intensity continued to increase at 525 nm. In addition, the color of Y-CDs solution changed from dark orange to bright yellow. When the water content exceeded 60 %, the FL signal at 525 nm was gradually decreased, and the color of Y-CDs solution changed to light yellow. Fig. 4f visually illustrates the spectral spectrum changes at 525 nm in the Y-CDs solution that contained varying amounts of water. These optical properties show that, Y-CDs possess good anti-interference performance and could have a potential application in bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of doping of rare earth elements on Y-CDs FL
ce:section.ce:para: When investigating the impact of doping rare earth elements Yb and Er on the FL of Y-CDs, the rare earth elements were eliminated from the raw materials. CDs were synthesized using TPE as the precursor through a solvent thermal method, which resulted in contrast CDs that are referred to as L-CDs.

When water was added to the L-CDs solution, the solution quickly precipitated, which formed a solid precipitate. Fig. S1 shows the FL spectra of the L-CDs solution and powder under 365 nm excitation. In addition, photographs of the L-CDs solution and powder under natural sunlight and 365 nm UV are provided. Following the addition of water, the L-CDs solution gradually transformed from a colorless and transparent state to an opaque white turbid liquid with blue FL.

The aggregated powder of Y-CDs and L-CDs were analyzed for their total luminous spectra and chromaticity coordinates to further explore the distinctive optical properties. Fig. S3a and S3b show the total luminous spectra of Y-CDs and L-CDs. Neither of the CDs showed dependence on excitation wavelength. L-CDs predominantly emitted light in the blue region, with the optimal emission occurring at around 460 nm, but the FL intensity was weaker than with Y-CDs. Chromaticity coordinates, were determined for both Y-CDs and L-CDs as defined by the International Commission on Illumination (CIE) (Fig. S3c, d). The chromaticity coordinates for Y-CDs were found to be (0.3459, 0.5531), indicating the color of the emitted light. In contrast, the chromaticity coordinates for L-CDs were (0.1622, 0.1804) and (0.5531). These results serve to further confirm the effective influence of doping rare earth elements Yb and Er on the FL properties of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature sensitivity and pH-responsive FL characteristics of Y-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The temperature sensitivity of Y-CDs was investigated during the experiment. There were variations in the FL intensity of Y-CDs within the temperature range of 20–100 °C. Fig. 5 a shows that, the intensity of the Y-CDs FL signal gradually decreased as the temperature increased. Fig. 5b visually demonstrates the subtle changes in FL signal at 525 nm, which shows the potential to use Y-CDs as a novel temperature-sensitive luminescent material.

This study also explored the FL response of Y-CDs to different pH value. As the pH value of the solution changed gradually from 1 to 9, the FL intensity of Y-CDs consistently decreased (Fig. 5c). In addition, Fig. 5d visually illustrates changes in the FL signal at 525 nm as a response to varying pH values. The linear range is far broader than the existing pH probes and fulfills the intracellular pH monitoring demands (Table S1 in Supporting information). The pH-responsiveness of Y-CDs can potentially be ascribed to the protonation-deprotonation of the amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of Y-CDs [34,35]. However, the pH sensing mechanism of Y-CDs is not yet fully understood, so further investigation is required.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and intracellular imaging of Y-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Experiments using the standardized WST-1 assay to assess the viability of HeLa cells, 4 T1 cells, and HEK293 cells following the addition of Y-CDs were conducted to evaluate the cytotoxicity of Y-CDs. Fig. S3a shows that the survival rate of HeLa cells remained above 85 % after 24 h of incubation with Y-CDs, even at a concentration of 500 μg/mL. Furthermore, when Y-CDs concentration was set to 50 μg/mL, the survival rate of HeLa cells exceeded 93 %. Fig.S 3b shows that the viability of 4 T1 cells remained above 82 % following 24 h of incubation with Y-CDs at varying concentrations. Similarly, Fig. S3c shows that the survival rate of HEK293 cells remained above 83 % after 24 h of incubation with Y-CDs at various concentrations. These results provide evidence that Y-CDs exhibit low cytotoxicity as a biomimetic material and exert minimal impact on cell survival. Therefore, Y-CDs have significant potential for biological imaging applications.

HeLa cells were chosen as the target cells for imaging studies to investigate the cellular imaging capability of Y-CDs. The experimental parameters, which included the imaging time and the optimal concentration of Y-CDs used for imaging, were systematically optimized before the cellular imaging experiments using HeLa cells were conducted.

The optimal imaging time for Y-CDs in HeLa cells was investigated, as can be seen in Fig. S4. HeLa cells were treated with 10 μg/mL Y-CDs, and FL was observed following different durations of staining. FL was observed in the cytoplasm after a min and after 5 min, the FL imaging position shifted, with the cytoplasmic FL disappearing and FL becoming observable in the nucleus. After 10 min, clear FL was observed in the nucleus. As time progressed from 10 to 60 min, FL intensity continued to increase. Fig. S6 provides a quantitative analysis of FL intensity, which demonstrates the effects Y-CDs incubation time has on HeLa cell imaging. In order to ensure stable and reliable staining, 30 min was chosen as the imaging time. The results indicates that CDs are not sensitive to imaging time, and a wide range of imaging times can be used. This also proves that Y-CDs are not demanding in terms of imaging incubation time and the selection range is relatively wide, which enables rapid imaging of 5 min. This may depend on its small particle size and low cytotoxicity and it can easily enter the cell and stay. Therefore, in comparison to the results in published work, the prepared Y-CDs in this study had much faster reaction times (Table S2 in Supporting information).

In addition to optimizing the imaging time, the impact varying concentrations of Y-CDs had on cellular imaging was examined. Concentrations that ranged from 1 μg/mL to 50 μg/mL were carefully chosen. Following a 30 min incubation period, the resulting microscope fluorescent diagram is shown in Fig. S7. The effect of Y-CDs on cell imaging was clearly observed at a concentration of 1 μg/mL. As the Y-CDs concentration increased, the FL intensity of HeLa cells also increased. Fluorescent images were utilized for the quantitative analysis of the changes in HeLa cell FL intensity as a function of Y-CDs concentration, as can be seen in Fig. S8. It is apparent that there was a significant increase in the fluorescent signal at a concentration of 10 μg/mL. Therefore, a concentration of 10 μg/mL was chosen for cell imaging experiments. Y-CDs are not dependent on high concentrations for imaging and the imaging effect is clear and stable at low concentrations. In addition, the effect of material toxicity on cells at concentrations that are too high is avoided.

A combination of the nuclear-specific commercial dye DAPI, the lysosome-specific commercial dye Lyso-Tracker Green, and 10 μg/mL Y-CDs was employed for cell staining as a means of investigating the cellular distribution pattern of Y-CDs in HeLa cells. Fig. 6 a shows that Y-CDs were predominantly localized in the nucleus, which resulted in yellow FL when combined with the blue DAPI staining. In contrast, Fig. 6b demonstrates that DAPI selectively stained the HeLa cell nucleus in blue. Fig. 6c visualizes the lysosomes in green using Lyso-Tracker Green dye. The merged fluorescent image that is presented in Fig. 6d shows the combined staining effect of Y-CDs, DAPI, and Lyso-Tracker Green, which provides a more pronounced visualization of these cellular components. These findings suggest that Y-CDs exhibit nucleus-targeting properties in HeLa cells and have minimal presence in the cytoplasm or lysosomes. The selective localization of Y-CDs in the nucleus serves to enhance their potential for application in nuclear imaging.

FL data was then subjected to statistical analysis using the “coloc2″ plugin in ImageJ software. The scatter plot that displayed the data points corresponding to Fig. 6a and 6b is shown in Fig. S9. This figure shows there to be an even distribution of data points on both sides of the diagonal line. In addition, the high Pearson correlation coefficient value of 0.99 proves there to be an excellent correlation between the overlapping fluorescent signals. The significant overlap and high colocalization coefficient that can be observed in the merged image provide further support for the specific distribution of Y-CDs in the nucleus of HeLa cells and the replacement of commercial dyes for labeling nuclei in cells.

Due to the particle size of Y-CDs, which Fig. 2 shows to be approximately 5 nm, it is quite plausible that Y-CDs are able to penetrate the nuclear pore with an approximate diameter of 10 nm. In addition, the surface of Y-CDs contains a large number of amino groups and carboxyl groups, all of which are positively charged. At the same time, the main backbone of the DNA double spiral structure includes phosphate groups and deoxyribose nucleotides. Following hydrolysis, the phosphate bases are negatively charged and through electrostatic interaction, the negatively charged regions in Y-CDs and DNA are able to combine with each other, which facilitates the specific distribution of Y-CDs in the nucleus.

The endocytosis mechanism of Y-CDs was investigated, with a specific focus on the energy dependency of cellular uptake. Previous studies have demonstrated that low temperature (4 °C) has the ability to impair ATP production in cells, which inhibits intracellular engulfment [36,37]. Cells were exposed to a low temperature of 4 °C for 2 h before incubation with Y-CDs in order to evaluate the significance of energy in cellular uptake. Fig. S10b shows there to be a notable decrease in FL intensity in comparison to the control group (Fig. S10a), which indicates that the cellular uptake of Y-CDs was energy-dependent.

Cellular uptake generally involves various mechanisms, including raft-mediated endocytosis, clathrin-mediated endocytosis, caveolae-mediated endocytosis, and macropinocytosis [38]. In order to further investigate the specific cellular uptake process of Y-CDs, cells were treated with inhibitors, which included CPZ (an inhibitor of clathrin-mediated endocytosis), genistein (an inhibitor of caveolae-mediated endocytosis), MβCD (an inhibitor of raft-mediated endocytosis), and amiloride (an inhibitor of macropinocytosis) for 2 h prior to incubation with Y-CDs. Comparative analysis was performed with the control group (Fig. S10a) and this revealed a significant decrease in FL intensity for HeLa cells that were treated with CPZ (Fig. S10c). The three remaining groups exhibited relatively weak FL signals (Figs. S10d-f), which suggests that clathrin-mediated endocytosis potentially plays a significant role in the cellular uptake of Y-CDs in HeLa cells. At the same time, it shows that caveolae-mediated endocytosis, raft-mediated endocytosis, and macropinocytosis may play a lesser role in this process.

The cellular uptake mechanism experiments of Y-CDs were subsequently further analyzed using fluorescent images, as can be seen in Fig. S11. The FL intensity of HeLa cells decreased significantly by 63.34 % following exposure to a low temperature (4 °C). In addition, treatment with CPZ caused a substantial reduction of 67.8 % in the FL intensity of HeLa cells. However, the three remaining groups exhibited minimal changes in FL intensity, which suggests that the main pathway for the cellular uptake of Y-CDs involves clathrin-mediated endocytosis. The significant FL intensity decrease with low temperature and CPZ treatment provides evidence of the involvement of energy-dependent mechanisms, particularly clathrin-mediated endocytosis, in the internalization of Y-CDs. In conclusion, the cellular uptake of Y-CDs is a process that is energy-dependent and is reliant on the internal swallowing effect of clathrin-mediated endocytosis. These findings serve to provide insights into the endocytic mechanism and cellular uptake behavior of Y-CDs in HeLa cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Y-CDs in zebrafish imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Zebrafish and zebrafish embryos were employed as vertebrate models to verify the applicability of Y-CDs in biological imaging. Zebrafish embryos were chosen because of their fast development and optical transparency, which allow for imaging purposes. Fig. 7 a1-a3 show zebrafish embryos and Fig. 7a4-a6 show zebrafish following incubation with 10 μg/mL Y-CDs for 30 min. Fig. 7a3 reveals the presence of FL in the zebrafish embryo, while the yolk sac exhibits no FL. This proves that Y-CDs have the ability to enter the embryo through the chorionic membrane. It is suggested by the difference in FL intensity between the yolk sac and the internal region of the embryo that Y-CDs exhibit varying degrees of affinity for different tissues in the embryo. Fig. 7af shows the accumulation of Y-CDs in the digestive system and yolk sac of the zebrafish. This shows that Y-CDs enter the body of the zebrafish through both swallowing and cutaneous absorption mechanisms [39–41]. These findings prove that Y-CDs can effectively penetrate zebrafish and zebrafish embryos and also have tissue affinity for juveniles, which provides a novel material choice for zebrafish imaging.

Zebrafish embryos at 12 h post-fertilization were incubated with 10 μg/mL Y-CDs for 30 min, followed by replacement with regular embryo medium to evaluate the long-term imaging capability and distribution of Y-CDs in zebrafish. Fluorescent images were captured at intervals of 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 7, and 10 days post-treatment as a means of examining the distribution pattern. Fig. 7b1 shows focused fluorescent images that were taken at 12 h following treatment with Y-CDs, together with an overlay image in Fig. 7b13. It can be seen that Y-CDs were uniformly distributed throughout the zebrafish embryo at this stage. Over the next 1–3 days, as the embryo developed the head and torso, the distribution of Y-CDs also extended to these regions. By the fifth day following carbon dot administration, Y-CDs were found to accumulate primarily in the intestinal bulb, intestines, and liver of the zebrafish, and a smaller amount was present in the head and torso (Fig. 7b5).

The focused fluorescent image in Fig. 7b5 shows there to be significant yellow FL in the posterior blood vessels on the seventh day, which indicates that Y-CDs had entered the circulatory system and distributed throughout the zebrafish. The extensive yellow FL observed in the trunk region and the newly appearing accumulation in the eyes further support this and suggest the high permeability of Y-CDs and their ability to cross the blood-eye barrier [42]. By the 10th day post-carbon dot treatment, Y-CDs were observed to reside primarily in the digestive system of the juvenile zebrafish, as can be seen in Fig. 7b6. It should be noted that only the lens of the eyes of the zebrafish emitted yellow FL at this stage, which can be easily distinguished from the eyeball area. Most importantly, no toxic effects on development were observed, which indicates that Y-CDs have excellent biocompatibility.

Y-CDs also exhibit stable FL emission, which is crucial for long-term imaging applications. In addition, their ability to cross the blood-eye barrier is evident from the accumulation of Y-CDs in the eyes. There is a similarity between the eye development of zebrafish and that of other vertebrates and the exceptional properties of Y-CDs suggest that they have potential for various applications in eye-related imaging. These characteristics include biocompatibility, stable FL emission, and the ability to cross biological barriers, which makes Y-CDs promising candidates for eye imaging and related applications.

Zebrafish embryos were continuously incubated with low (10 μg/mL) and high (100 μg/mL) concentrations of Y-CDs for a duration of 14 days to assess the biological safety of Y-CDs for imaging applications. Confocal microscopy was used to capture and analyze fluorescent images (Fig. 7c). The accumulation of Y-CDs in the yolk sac of developing zebrafish embryos was observed at both concentrations. No apparent abnormalities in zebrafish development were observed when they were subjected to low-concentration incubation for 14 days (Fig. 7c3). This suggests that long-term incubation with a low concentration of Y-CDs does not impact the development of zebrafish and provides clear and stable imaging, which highlights the biocompatible nature of Y-CDs.

However, when they were exposed to high concentrations of Y-CDs, there was notable edema in the yolk sac (indicated by the red box) and pericardial region (indicated by the red circle) of the zebrafish embryos (Fig. 7c6). This suggests that long-term incubation with high concentrations of Y-CDs may result in yolk sac and heart deformities in zebrafish [43,44]. These findings indicate that low concentrations of Y-CDs have no apparent adverse effects on the development of zebrafish and provide clear imaging, but high concentrations can cause deformities, so caution is required in their application. Careful dosage and concentration control are essential for ensuring safe and effective Y-CDs use for biological imaging purposes.

In conclusion, a novel type of carbon dot (Y-CDs) was successfully designed and synthesized by incorporating rare earth elements, which exhibited both aggregation-induced emission (AIE) and yellow FL emission when they were exposed to UV light following the addition of water. The optical properties of Y-CDs were thoroughly studied and the influence rare earth element doping has on these properties was investigated and confirmed. It is notable that Y-CDs demonstrated excellent temperature sensitivity and pH response characteristics. There was a stable decrease in FL intensity as the temperature increased between 20 and 100 °C, and a decrease in FL intensity as pH values increased in the range of 1–9.

Y-CDs were successfully used for imaging applications, which included the visualization of HeLa cells and zebrafish embryos. The intracellular internalization mechanism of Y-CDs in HeLa cells was investigated and successful cell nucleus imaging was achieved. Y-CDs exhibited long-term imaging capabilities in zebrafish embryos and specific targeting of the lens was observed in zebrafish eyes and juvenile fish. This suggests that there is potential for Y-CDs application in the field of biological imaging.

Generally, due to their unique optical properties, biocompatibility, and specific targeting abilities, the synthesized Y-CDs with rare earth elements showed great promise for a variety of imaging and biological sensing applications. Further research and development in this area could result in the advancement of bioimaging techniques and the exploration of new applications in biology and medicine.

Xinyue Kou: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yingge Cong: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Wen-Fei Dong: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Li Li: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (2022YFC2403500), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82172077, 22201298, 82071066, and 62027825), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2022QB077 and ZR2022QB243), and the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Team of Jiangsu Province (JSSCTD202145).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2024.112855.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
